author: Macmill-340
text:
MAKING DECISIONS IN CODE
An if statement lets your program choose what to do based on whether a condition is true or false.

SYNTAX:
    if condition:
        # code that runs if condition is True
    else:
        # code that runs if condition is False

THE COLON AND INDENTATION
The colon after the condition is required — it tells Python the condition has ended and a block is starting.
Every line inside the block must be indented (4 spaces is the standard).

    temperature = 35
    if temperature > 30:
        print("It is hot")     # indented — inside the if block
    else:
        print("Not too hot")   # indented — inside the else block

COMPARISON OPERATORS:
    ==    equal to           (x == 5)
    !=    not equal to       (x != 5)
    >     greater than       (x > 5)
    <     less than          (x < 5)
    >=    greater or equal   (x >= 5)
    <=    less or equal      (x <= 5)

CRITICAL: = vs ==
    =   is assignment — stores a value:   score = 100
    ==  is comparison — checks equality:  score == 100

Using = inside an if condition is a SyntaxError:
    if score = 100:     # Wrong
    if score == 100:    # Correct

ELIF
Use elif to check more conditions if the first was false:
    if score >= 90:
        print("Excellent")
    elif score >= 60:
        print("Passing")
    else:
        print("Try again")
common_errors: Using a single = inside an if statement (if x = 5: is a SyntaxError). Use == to compare. Also: forgetting the colon at the end of the if or else line.
task: Create a variable named 'temperature' with the value 35. Write an if/else statement: if temperature is greater than 30, print 'Too hot'. Otherwise, print 'Just right'.
